stories.headline: Trump threatens to leave NATO and lashes out at European allies for not joining Iran war
stories.body: Donald Trump launched a tirade against European countries that refused to join the war against Iran, telling governments worried about fuel prices to go get your own oil by force from the Gulf. On Truth Social, he singled out the United Kingdom for refusing to get involved and accused France of being very unhelpful after Paris blocked Israeli planes from transporting American weapons through its airspace. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Washington would reassess whether NATO has served this country well or become a one-way street. France and Italy both emerged as having quietly restricted military flights connected to the Iran war, with Rome denying US bombers last-minute landing permission in Sicily and Paris requiring case-by-case authorization for combat-related overflights. Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth mocked the Royal Navy, asking where the big bad fleet was when it came to securing the Strait of Hormuz. The UK, which has allowed the US to use its bases despite calling the war illegal, found itself publicly admonished from both sides of the Atlantic.

stories.headline: Trump claims Iran's president asked for ceasefire and says US forces will leave within two to three weeks
stories.body: President Trump said US military action in Iran could end within two to three weeks, claiming Iranian president Masoud Pezeshkian had asked for a ceasefire, though he added that a deal was not necessary for the US to withdraw. Trump said the US had achieved its primary goal of setting back Iran's nuclear capability by 15 to 20 years and destroyed its navy, military and air force. The White House announced Trump would address the nation on Wednesday evening to provide an important update on Iran. Secretary of State Rubio confirmed talks are ongoing and said there is potential for a direct meeting at some point. However, Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi told Al Jazeera that no negotiations are under way despite exchanges of messages, while the IRGC said the Strait of Hormuz would not be opened by ridiculous displays of the US president. Foreign policy analysts noted Trump has repeatedly pushed back his timeline for ending the conflict, having initially promised it would be over in four days, then three weeks, and now another two to three weeks.

stories.headline: Russian military plane crash kills 29 in occupied Crimea
stories.body: A Russian An-26 military transport aircraft crashed in the occupied Ukrainian peninsula of Crimea on Tuesday, killing all 29 people on board including seven crew members and 22 passengers. The Russian defence ministry blamed a technical failure and reported no external damage to the aircraft, implying that missiles, drones or birds were not suspected of causing the crash. Russia's Investigative Committee opened an inquiry into a flight safety violation. The An-26 is a Soviet-era aircraft manufactured by the Ukrainian company Antonov that has been in use since the late 1960s and has been involved in several deadly crashes, including one that killed 26 people in Kharkiv in 2020 and another that killed 28 in the Russian Far East in 2021. Fighting between Ukrainian and Russian forces has been ongoing in Crimea since Moscow's full-scale invasion four years ago, with Ukrainian strikes largely targeting Russian military bases in the peninsula.

stories.headline: NASA begins fueling Artemis II rocket for first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years
stories.body: NASA began fueling its Space Launch System rocket on Wednesday for the Artemis II mission, which will send four astronauts around the Moon for the first time since Apollo 17 in 1972. The launch team needed to load more than 700,000 gallons of hydrogen and oxygen fuel into the 32-story rocket at Kennedy Space Center before commander Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Christina Koch and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen could board. Glover will become the first Black astronaut to travel to the Moon, and Koch the first woman. The crew will fly around the Moon without stopping or orbiting, setting a new distance record for the farthest humans have traveled from Earth as they zoom some 4,000 miles beyond the lunar surface before heading back for a Pacific splashdown. Tensions were high after dangerous hydrogen leaks during a countdown test earlier this year forced a lengthy delay.

stories.headline: American journalist Shelly Kittleson kidnapped in Baghdad by suspected Iran-backed militia
stories.body: American freelance journalist Shelly Kittleson was kidnapped in Baghdad by members of a suspected Iranian-backed Iraqi armed group, the US State Department confirmed. Surveillance video showed a man forcing Kittleson into a car on a Baghdad street before the vehicle drove off. Iraqi authorities intercepted the suspects' vehicle when it overturned during a pursuit, arresting one individual with ties to the militia group Kataib Hezbollah, while the search continued for the remaining kidnappers. Kittleson is a longtime reporter in the region who has covered conflicts in Syria, Iraq and Lebanon for outlets including Al-Monitor, which called for her safe and immediate release. The abduction highlights the deteriorating security situation in Iraq since the US-Iran war began, with pro-Iran militias targeting the US embassy and military bases while the US has carried out repeated strikes against the groups.

stories.headline: Supreme Court hears landmark challenge to birthright citizenship as Trump makes unprecedented visit
stories.body: The Supreme Court heard oral arguments on Wednesday in a constitutional challenge to President Trump's executive order attempting to deny citizenship to children born in the United States to parents who are in the country illegally or temporarily. Trump made an unprecedented visit to the court to attend the arguments in person, the first sitting president to do so. The 14th Amendment and federal law are widely understood to make citizens of everyone born in the country, with narrow exceptions for children of foreign diplomats and enemy forces. The case, which could be decided this summer, represents the most significant legal test of birthright citizenship in over a century, reaching back to the 1898 Supreme Court ruling in United States v. Wong Kim Ark that established the principle. Pro and anti-Trump demonstrators rallied outside the courthouse as the justices heard the arguments.

stories.headline: Trump signs executive order creating national voter registry and restricting mail-in voting
stories.body: President Trump signed an executive order to create a nationwide list of verified eligible voters and restrict mail-in voting, a move that immediately drew legal threats from Democratic state officials. The order, which voting law experts say violates the Constitution by attempting to seize states' power to run elections, is the latest in Trump's efforts to reshape voting based on his false allegations of fraud. While signing the order, Trump repeated his debunked claims about mail-in ballot cheating, asserting again that he won three times. Numerous audits, investigations and court rulings have found no evidence of widespread voter fraud in any recent US election. The executive order comes ahead of this year's midterm elections and is expected to face immediate legal challenges.

stories.headline: Global scramble to contain oil and gas prices falls short as Iran war disrupts supplies
stories.body: Stopgap measures by world leaders have failed to halt the surge in oil and gasoline prices triggered by the Iran war, which took a record amount of oil off the market when tankers were stranded in the Persian Gulf and military strikes damaged refineries and export terminals. US petrol prices passed an average of four dollars a gallon as the EU's energy commissioner warned that prices would not return to normal any time soon, even if peace were declared tomorrow. A group of 32 nations began releasing the largest volume of emergency oil reserves in the International Energy Agency's history at 400 million barrels, while Trump tapped the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, lifted sanctions on Russian and Iranian crude, and temporarily waived the Jones Act to allow foreign-flagged ships to carry oil between US ports. Australia's prime minister addressed the nation and announced Covid-era support measures as his treasurer warned small businesses were paying the price for the conflict.

stories.headline: Airport bottlenecks ease as TSA workers receive backpay during ongoing government shutdown
stories.body: Frustrating security lines at US airports dwindled on Monday as Transportation Security Administration officers began receiving backpay for working during the government shutdown. Checkpoint lines that had stretched to four hours at Houston's George Bush Intercontinental Airport shrank to waits of 10 minutes or less. In other previous trouble spots including Atlanta and Baltimore-Washington International Airport, travelers were moving smoothly to their flights. However, the government shutdown itself continues with no resolution in sight, and ICE officers remained stationed at airports. The improvements came as airlines raised prices to offset rising fuel costs from the Iran war, with JetBlue increasing checked bag fees by as much as nine dollars.

stories.headline: Indra president Angel Escribano set to resign as emergency board meeting called amid government pressure
stories.body: Angel Escribano, president of Spain's main defence company Indra, is expected to resign on Wednesday afternoon after an emergency board meeting was called for 5 PM, surprising many board members during the Semana Santa holiday. The move comes after intense pressure from the Spanish government, which lost confidence in the executive through the SEPI state holding company. Escribano, who holds over 14 percent of Indra, had refused to step down last week when SEPI flagged a conflict of interest over a proposed acquisition of his family company Escribano Mechanical and Engineering. While the brothers withdrew that deal, the situation became untenable at a critical moment for Spain's defence industry, with Indra designated as the country's flagship defence contractor. Indra shares fell around 4 percent on the news while the broader Spanish market rose approximately 3 percent.

stories.headline: PP and Vox approve parliamentary investigation linking terrorism risk to mass immigration regularization
stories.body: Spain's Popular Party and Vox used their combined majority in the joint congressional security committee to approve a study investigating the government's planned mass regularization of undocumented immigrants, framing it as a terrorism risk. PP deputy Rafael Hernando argued the process would allow any criminal or terrorist to enter Spain and Europe, while party spokesperson Ester Munoz called the regularization a security hole for the entire EU. The move follows PP leader Alberto Nunez Feijoo's remarks at a European People's Party summit in Brussels where he linked the regularization to heightened terrorist alert levels during the Iran war. PSOE, ERC and EH Bildu voted against the initiative. The government's draft decree would open application windows at post offices and Social Security offices for an estimated half a million to one million undocumented people currently in Spain.

stories.headline: France and Italy quietly join Spain in restricting military flights linked to Iran war
stories.body: France and Italy have both imposed restrictions on military flights connected to the war in the Middle East, joining Spain in pushing back against the US-led campaign. France blocked Israeli planes from using its airspace to transport American weapons destined for the Iran conflict, requiring case-by-case authorization for combat-related overflights, while continuing to allow routine American flights under international norms. Italy denied US bombers last-minute landing permission at the Sigonella air base in Sicily because there was insufficient time to consult parliament as required under bilateral agreements. Neither country had publicly announced the restrictions before Trump attacked France on Truth Social, accusing it of being very unhelpful. The Elysee Palace responded that France had not changed its position since the first day of the conflict. The measures effectively create a growing European buffer zone against the Iran war, complicating US and Israeli logistics across the Mediterranean.

stories.headline: Spain to close fast-track regularization path that granted 240,000 permits to Venezuelans since 2018
stories.body: Spain will end in June the special mechanism that since 2018 has allowed tens of thousands of Venezuelans to regularize their status almost automatically through humanitarian residence permits. The system, which granted around 240,000 authorizations over eight years and accounted for over 95 percent of all humanitarian permits in some years, had become a structural channel rather than the temporary exception it was intended to be. Venezuelans who applied for asylum were routinely given 12-month renewable humanitarian residence permits that allowed them to work and eventually pursue permanent residency and citizenship. Government sources described the change as procedural rather than substantive, saying humanitarian cases would be handled through a new channel, but in practice the move significantly complicates the path for Venezuelans considering emigrating to Spain as humanitarian permits are typically reserved for very specific medical circumstances.

stories.headline: Mossos investigate Islamophobic chants at Spain-Egypt match as FIFA sanction looms for Spanish football federation
stories.body: The Mossos d'Esquadra have opened an investigation into Islamophobic chants during the Spain-Egypt friendly at the RCDE Stadium in Cornella, where a section of the crowd repeatedly chanted the equivalent of anyone who is not jumping is Muslim. The chants were clearly audible on television broadcasts and prompted swift condemnation from the Catalan government, the Spanish football federation and the Ministry of Education and Sports. Lamine Yamal, the Spanish national team's star player, responded publicly saying he is Muslim and called the chants an intolerable lack of respect. Under FIFA's disciplinary code, the federation faces a likely fine and potentially a match behind closed doors, following precedents including Hungary's 216,000-euro fine and closed-door match after racist abuse toward England players in 2021. The Bulgarian referee did not understand the Spanish-language chants during the match, so the anti-racism protocol was not activated. Espanyol, whose stadium hosted the match, condemned the racism while denouncing what it called a gratuitous campaign to discredit its fans.

stories.headline: Catalunya's tourist tax doubles in Barcelona and rises across the region as Semana Santa begins
stories.body: A significant increase in Catalunya's tourist tax took effect on Wednesday, coinciding with the start of Semana Santa holiday travel. In Barcelona, the tax has doubled, meaning a night in a five-star hotel now costs 7 euros in regional tax per person instead of 3.50, on top of Barcelona city's separate surcharge which rises to 5 euros and will increase by one euro annually until reaching 8 euros in 2029. Outside Barcelona, the increase is being phased in over two years, with a five-star hotel or luxury campsite costing 4.50 euros per night now and rising to 6 euros from April 2027. The tax was approved by the Catalan parliament in February through a pact between PSC, ERC and the Comuns, with opposition from Junts, PP, Vox and Alianca. Twenty-five percent of the revenue goes to housing policies, with the remainder split between the Generalitat and municipalities for tourism promotion and infrastructure. The tax has been in place since 2012 but the sharp increase has drawn criticism from the hospitality sector.

stories.headline: Catalan Parliament to expand into historic 1888 Universal Exhibition building near the Zoo
stories.body: The Catalan Parliament will gain approximately 8,000 square metres of new space in an expansion across the Ciutadella park, occupying the current Guardia Urbana stables in a building originally designed for the 1888 Universal Exhibition. Parliament president Josep Rull, Generalitat president Salvador Illa and Barcelona mayor Jaume Collboni signed a cooperation protocol on Tuesday to formalize the project, which aims to address the institution's cramped conditions where most deputies currently share offices. The new building at Carrer Wellington, 300 metres from the main parliament palace, was originally the Gallery of Machines designed by architect Adria Casademont i Vidal to display railway machinery at the 1888 exposition. Only its main facade and a small portion of the original halls survive. The Guardia Urbana horses will be relocated to Collserola. The project is expected to take six to eight years and forms part of a broader plan for the Ciutadella area that includes a CSIC biosciences centre and Barcelona's Central Urban Library.

stories.headline: Lloret de Mar resolves 17-year dispute over coastal path blocked by Kazakh magnate's estate
stories.body: After 17 years of legal battles, more than 15 court rulings and multiple protests, the town of Lloret de Mar has reached an agreement with the owners of the Can Juncadella estate to reopen the coastal path connecting Cala Canyelles and Cala Morisca. The path has been blocked since 2008 when the property, owned by a former president of Kazakhstan and built within the protected natural area of the Cadiretes massif, closed public access. Under the deal, the path will be rerouted closer to the shoreline while remaining within the property boundary, and the estate will bear the full cost of design and construction. Walkers who attempted to use the path over the years encountered trees blocking the way, surveillance cameras, warnings about dangerous dogs, megaphone announcements and photography bans. Environmental groups expressed concern that the agreement, which must still be approved by a full council vote in late April, does not guarantee the same thing will not happen again since the new route still crosses private land.
